patches.rationale: WIN-028 has no monitoring code at all — there is no monitor.py entry for the Bermuda/Japan geographic symmetry claim. c2 and c3 reviews both flagged this; c2 explicitly recommended setting to 'none'. The 'hardcoded' tag implies a script that hardcodes a target value, which does not exist for this WIN.

patches.find: Insurance data, NOAA, and USCG confirm no anomalous loss rates. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patches.replace: Insurance data, NOAA, and USCG confirm no anomalous loss rates. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on temporal grounds. The Bermuda Triangle and Devil's Sea are named folklore regions first popularised in the 1950s–1970s — their geographic coordinates appear on nautical charts predating the dome model by centuries. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the dome did not derive these region locations from dome geometry — it adopted pre-existing folklore zones whose coordinates were long established and declared them consistent with dome predictions. No monitor.py entry exists for this WIN; there is no script and no timestamped numerical prediction to evaluate. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the confirming material be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for centuries-old cartographic coordinates and mid-20th-century magazine articles.
patches.rationale: WIN-028 has no monitoring code, so the generic 'monitor script hardcodes the target number' boilerplate is factually wrong. c3 review flagged this as a major internal contradiction. Replaced with WIN-028-specific post-hoc argument framing the temporal priority of the folklore region coordinates. Also fixes NOAA generic homepage link to the specific Bermuda Triangle page.

patches.find: NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan anomaly zones show 180° geomagnetic symmetry as predicted by dome geometry. Part (1) is debunked above. Part (2) conflates geographic folklore with geophysics — the Bermuda region is not a geomagnetic anomaly (the South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major geomagnetic feature, is centered off the coast of Brazil, not Bermuda). The Japan region ("Devil's Sea") likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature. The dome's claimed 180° symmetry does not correspond to any pattern in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models.
patches.replace: NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan anomaly zones show 180° geomagnetic symmetry as predicted by dome geometry. Part (1) is debunked above. Part (2) conflates geographic folklore with geophysics — the Bermuda region is not a geomagnetic anomaly (the South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major geomagnetic feature, is centered off the coast of Brazil, not Bermuda). The Japan region ("Devil's Sea") likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature. The dome's claimed 180° symmetry does not correspond to any pattern in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models. Quantitatively: the Bermuda region (~25°N, 71°W) and Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) are separated by 152° of longitude on the short arc (208° on the long arc) — 28° off the claimed 180°, a 15.6% deviation that exceeds the model's own tolerance thresholds elsewhere. Neither agonic line passes through its associated region: the western agonic at ~80°W only grazes the Bermuda Triangle's western edge, and the eastern agonic at ~100–120°E lies 17–37° west of the Devil's Sea (137°E).
patches.rationale: Adds the concrete 152°/208° longitude-separation arithmetic (c2 moderate, c3 still unfixed) and the agonic-line position data (c2 minor, c3 still unfixed). Also fixes NOAA generic homepage link to the specific Bermuda Triangle fact page. These make the geomagnetic refutation concrete and harder to handwave.

patches.find: The dome's nearby moon at 2,534 km would produce one sharp local tidal pulse, not the observed global two-bulge pattern.
patches.replace: The dome's nearby moon at 2,534 km would produce a sharp localized tidal pulse beneath its circuit path, not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed globally.
patches.rationale: O1 is a diurnal constituent, not semidiurnal. The two-bulge image (near-side + far-side) describes M2 (semidiurnal). WIN-050's correct argument is that the dome's local moon produces a localized spike rather than the gentle global diurnal modulation required for O1. c3 review flagged this as a major TLDR factual error.

patches.find: Tabulated since 1921. The dome's local moon can't produce the two-bulge pattern actually observed globally.
patches.replace: Tabulated since 1921. The dome's local moon can't produce the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation O1 requires — only a localized spike beneath its circuit path.
patches.rationale: Same diurnal/semidiurnal conflation as tldr_evidence; two-bulge framing applies to M2 not O1. c3 review flagged both TLDRs.

patches.find: Dome's local moon at 2,534 km produces one sharp tidal pulse, not the observed two-bulge global pattern.
patches.replace: Dome's local moon at 2,534 km produces one sharp tidal pulse, not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed globally.
patches.rationale: Same conflation — two-bulge describes M2 (semidiurnal), not O1 (diurnal). The correct contrast is spike vs. smooth diurnal modulation. c3 review flagged this in detail_verdict_text.

patches.find: dropping to 9% of peak at 5,000 km offset and 0.6% at the equator
patches.replace: dropping to 9% of peak at 5,000 km offset and 1.48% at the equator
patches.rationale: WIN-050's '0.6% at the equator' is inconsistent with WIN-049's '1.48% at the equator' for the same dome-moon geometry (h=2,534 km, equator distance R≈10,000 km). The canonical 1/r³ falloff gives ratio=(h/d)³ where d=sqrt(R²+h²) → 1.48%. c3 moderate finding. Aligning with WIN-049 eliminates a cross-WIN inconsistency a careful reader would notice.

patches.replace: concentrates force in a localized cone beneath the sub-lunar track
patches.rationale: The dome moon at 2,534 km produces a force cone ~5,000 km wide at 9% intensity, not literally a point. 'At one point' overstates localization and is factually imprecise. c3 minor finding (also affects the summary table). Replacement matches the recommended phrasing from the c2 review.
